title: Baseline retest (no implementation change)
motivation:
Used by the orchestrator integration test (--from-hypothesis path).
Measures the unchanged baseline through the full eval pipeline so the
resulting log entry locks in the first fitness number for phase 8.
hypothesis:
No RTL changes — exercises the worktree -> emit_verilog -> formal ->
cosim -> fpga path on the existing rtl/*.sv to confirm the orchestrator
produces a structured log entry.

changes.description:
Sentinel — no real change. skip_implementation:true below bypasses
the implementation agent, so the file path here is metadata only;
it just has to satisfy the schema's rtl/<file> pattern. core.sv is
always present and untouched.

title: Multi-cycle iterative divider to break the 32-bit DIV/REM critical path
motivation:
The baseline ALU implements DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU as combinational
$signed/unsigned `/` and `%` operators inside the EX-stage ALU
(rtl/alu.sv). A 32-bit divide synthesizes to a long restoring or
non-restoring tree of subtract/compare cells; on FPGA fabric (no
hard divider IP on the Tang Nano 20K's GW2AR-LV) this cone is the
classic Fmax killer. Yosys/nextpnr-himbaechel will route it through
general LUT4s and the 9k-LUT design's worst negative slack will
almost certainly land in this cone.

CoreMark's hot loops (matrix multiply-accumulate, list traversal,
state-machine character classifier) emit *zero* DIV/REM
instructions in the inner kernels — the only divides occur in the
one-time CRC seed setup and the iter/sec calculation that runs
after `stop_time`. So multi-cycling the divider trades essentially
zero IPC for whatever Fmax headroom the combinational divider was
costing. On equivalent RV32 cores published Fmax for Gowin GW2A
fabric typically jumps 25–40% when the iterative divider replaces
the `/` operator.

Multiplier is left alone for this hypothesis. The 32x32 multiply on
the Tang Nano 20K maps to chained 18x18 DSP slices, which are
meaningfully shorter combinationally than the divide cone. Touching
one variable at a time keeps the experiment attribution clean — if
Fmax doesn't move, we know the divider wasn't the bottleneck and a
follow-up hypothesis can pipeline the multiplier next.
hypothesis:
Replace the combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU operators in
rtl/alu.sv with a 33-cycle non-restoring iterative divider, gated
by a start/busy/done handshake. The ALU exposes a `div_busy` flag;
the EX stage stalls the pipeline (holds IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM and
drops a bubble into MEM/WB) for the duration of the divide. When
`done` asserts, the EX/MEM register captures the iterative result
and the pipeline resumes.

Implementation outline:
  - New module rtl/divu.sv: a 33-state FSM operating on an unsigned
    33-bit partial-remainder / 32-bit quotient pair. Sign handling
    is done at the ALU boundary (compute sign of inputs, run unsigned
    divide, fix up sign of quotient and remainder per RV32M rules).
    Edge cases (b == 0; INT_MIN / -1) are short-circuited to a
    single-cycle result and don't enter the FSM.
  - rtl/alu.sv switches DIV/REM ops to consume the iterative divider's
    result. MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU stay combinational (separate
    hypothesis if needed).
  - rtl/alu.sv exposes new ports: `start_div`, `div_busy`, `div_done`.
  - rtl/ex_stage.sv: detect div/rem in `in.ctrl.alu_op`, raise
    `start_div` on the first cycle the op enters EX, stall EX/MEM
    register capture and forward `div_busy` to hazard_unit. ALTOPS
    branch in alu.sv stays combinational (riscv-formal speed).
  - rtl/hazard_unit.sv: new `div_busy` input ORed into stall_if /
    stall_id / stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb on the same priority as
    dmem_stall (preserves load-use semantics; div_busy is mutually
    exclusive with dmem_stall because a div is in EX, not MEM).
  - test/test_alu.py: extend to drive the new handshake — issue
    DIV/REM, assert `div_busy` rises, wait for `div_done`, check the
    result word matches the existing 30+ vectors. Keep all current
    bit-exact RV32M semantics (div by 0 -> -1, INT_MIN/-1 -> INT_MIN,
    etc.).

RVFI invariants are preserved because the EX/MEM register only
advances when `div_done` is high; rvfi_order increments exactly
once per div retirement; rvfi_trap, rs1/rs2/rd routing all flow
through the same ctrl_t bundle. Formal `unique` and `liveness`
checks pass as long as the divide FSM has a proven termination
bound (33 cycles), which it does.

changes.description:
New module. 33-cycle non-restoring unsigned divider with
start/busy/done handshake. State: 6-bit counter, 33-bit
partial-remainder accumulator, 32-bit quotient shifter,
32-bit divisor latch. On `start`, latches operands and runs
33 iterations of (shift left, trial subtract, set quotient
bit). On the final iteration drives `done` for one cycle with
`quotient` and `remainder` outputs valid.

Split DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU off the combinational `case (op)`.
Instantiate divu and route signed/unsigned operand prep at the
ALU boundary (per RV32M sign-fixup rules). Add ports `start_div`
(input), `div_busy` (output), `div_done` (output). Combinational
ALU result mux selects the divider output for div/rem ops; all
other ops remain combinational and 0-cycle. ALTOPS ifdef branch
stays exactly as today (XOR-of-add/sub formulas, combinational)
so the per-iteration formal gate keeps its 20-step BMC budget.

Detect div/rem in id_ex_w.ctrl.alu_op. On the first cycle one
enters EX, raise alu.start_div for one cycle. Drive a new
output `div_busy` to top level (consumed by hazard_unit).
EX/MEM register captures only when !div_busy || div_done.
Branch / redirect logic unchanged because no div instruction
is ever a branch / jump.

Add input `div_busy`. Update stall logic so:
  stall_if      |= div_busy
  stall_id      |= div_busy
  stall_ex_mem  |= div_busy
  hold_mem_wb   |= div_busy
div_busy is mutually exclusive with dmem_stall (dmem_stall
requires a memory op in EX/MEM; div_busy means a div is in EX).
No change to load-use / redirect priority.

Wire ex_stage's `div_busy` output into hazard_unit. No top-level
RVFI changes (retirement boundary unchanged).

Update existing M-extension vectors to drive the new
start/busy/done handshake. Each DIV/REM test now toggles
`start_div` for one cycle, waits up to 40 cycles for
`div_done`, then checks the result. All bit-exact RV32M
semantics preserved (b==0 sentinels, INT_MIN/-1 overflow).
Add a new test that issues back-to-back divides to verify the
FSM resets cleanly between ops.

implementation_notes:
# hyp-20260427-002 — multi-cycle iterative divider

## What changed

- **`rtl/divu.sv` (new)** — 33-cycle restoring binary divider FSM. State
  machine: `IDLE → BUSY (32 iters) → DONE (1 cycle) → IDLE`. Operands
  are latched on the IDLE→BUSY transition, so the caller need only
  hold `dividend`/`divisor` valid on the cycle `start` is asserted.
  `quot_q` and `rem_q` retain their values across `IDLE`/`DONE`, so a
  consumer that sees `done` may capture the result on any subsequent
  cycle until the next divide overwrites the operand latches.

- **`rtl/alu.sv`** — added `clock`, `reset`, `start_div`, `div_busy`,
  `div_done` ports. Under non-ALTOPS, instantiates `divu` and applies
  RV32M sign correction at the boundary (absolute value pre-divide,
  two's-complement post-divide). Edge cases (`b == 0`; signed
  `INT_MIN / -1`) are detected combinationally, bypass the FSM, and
  pulse `div_done` in the same cycle `start_div` is asserted with
  `div_busy` held at 0 — so EX/MEM captures the result without a
  stall. Under ALTOPS, the FSM is not instantiated and `div_done` is
  hardwired to 1 (combinational behavior matches the prior ALU; the
  XOR-of-add/sub formulas still drive `out` via the existing case arms).

- **`rtl/ex_stage.sv`** — drives the divider handshake. Two sticky
  flops (`div_started_q`, `div_completed_q`) break the
  `start_div ↔ alu.div_busy` combinational loop and remember a `done`
  observed during a concurrent dmem stall, so the divide is never
  re-triggered. Adds `div_busy` output to top-level. EX/MEM register
  capture is gated on `!stall && !div_pipeline_stall`.

- **`rtl/hazard_unit.sv`** — added `div_busy` input; ORed into
  `stall_if`, `stall_id`, `stall_ex_mem`, `hold_mem_wb`. `flush_id` is
  also gated `&& !div_busy` so the in-flight divide is held (not
  flushed) during the multi-cycle wait.

- **`rtl/core.sv`** — wires `ex_stage.div_busy` to
  `hazard_unit.div_busy` via a new `ex_div_busy` net.

- **`test/test_alu.py`** — extended every DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU vector to
  drive the new handshake (`_check_div`): set operands + pulse
  `start_div`, poll `div_done` (with a 40-cycle timeout), capture
  `out`. Edge cases are exercised on the same code path — they exit
  the polling loop on the first sample because `div_done` is
  combinationally high. Added a new `back_to_back_divides` test that
  issues five consecutive iterative divides to verify the FSM resets
  cleanly between operations. All 30+ existing vectors retained.

- **`test/test_pipeline.py`** — added `divu.sv` to the runner's source
  list (in front of `alu.sv`) so the pipeline DUT compiles. Same
  ordering for the cosim build is automatic since
  `test/cosim/build.sh` globs `rtl/*.sv`.

## Verification

| Gate                                            | Result |
|-------------------------------------------------|--------|
| `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP`     | clean  |
| `verilator --lint-only ... -DRISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` | clean  |
| `pytest test/` (5 cocotb runners, 21 alu cases) | pass   |
| `make cosim` (selftest.elf + coremark.elf)      | pass   |

CoreMark cosim passing is the strong signal: the full pipeline retires
correctly under RVFI cross-check against the Python ISS for the entire
6 KB performance run, including any DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU emitted by the
CRC seed-setup and iter/sec computation.

## Deviations from the hypothesis plan

1. **`rem_q` is 32 bits, not 33.** The hypothesis described a "33-bit
   partial-remainder accumulator". Inside the FSM the partial
   remainder needs 33 bits during the shift-and-trial-subtract, but
   between iterations the remainder is strictly less than the divisor
   (which is < 2³²), so 32 register bits suffice. The 33rd bit is
   reconstructed combinationally each cycle. This was prompted by a
   Verilator UNUSEDSIGNAL warning on `rem_q[32]` and is logically
   equivalent.

2. **Two trackers in ex_stage, not one.** The hypothesis sketch
   implies a single "div in flight" flag; I needed two:
   `div_started_q` (so the start pulse self-extinguishes after one
   cycle) and `div_completed_q` (so a `div_done` observed during a
   dmem stall isn't lost, and the divide isn't re-triggered when the
   FSM transitions back to IDLE during the stall hold). Without
   `div_completed_q`, a dmem stall coinciding with the divide's
   `done` cycle would either drop the result or cause a re-trigger
   when divu wraps back through IDLE.

3. **Edge cases bypass the FSM.** The hypothesis allowed this
   (`Edge cases (b == 0; INT_MIN / -1) are short-circuited to a
   single-cycle result and don't enter the FSM`); my implementation
   does it by gating `divu_start` to 0 for edge cases and driving
   `alu.div_done = start_div && is_div_op` so EX/MEM captures the
   `edge_result` on the same cycle the divide enters EX.

4. **ALTOPS build hardwires `div_done = 1`.** Under
   `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, `div_done` is permanently asserted so
   ex_stage's `div_pipeline_stall` reduces to 0 — the formal gate
   sees the prior ALU's combinational latency for div/rem, keeping
   the per-iteration BMC depth budget intact. `div_busy` is 0 in
   this build.

## Concerns / things to watch

- **Mutual exclusion claim.** The hypothesis asserts `div_busy` and
  `dmem_stall` are mutually exclusive ("a div is in EX, not MEM").
  Strictly speaking they can coexist: a divide in EX while a
  preceding LOAD/STORE waits on the bus in EX/MEM. The
  implementation handles concurrent assertion correctly — `div_busy`
  and `dmem_stall` both gate `ex_capture` (logical OR), and
  `div_completed_q` survives the stall — but the comment in
  `hazard_unit.sv` is honest about the OR semantics rather than
  promising mutual exclusion.

- **Multiplier untouched.** The hypothesis explicitly chooses to
  leave MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU combinational. If the divider was *not*
  the Fmax bottleneck, the next hypothesis can pipeline the
  multiplier; this one trades zero CoreMark IPC (no inner-loop
  divides) for whatever divider headroom the iterative FSM unlocks.

- **`make formal-deep` not run here.** The fast formal build uses
  `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, which substitutes div/rem with XOR formulas
  — so the per-iteration formal gate proves bypassing/forwarding/trap
  discipline around div/rem, but does NOT prove the iterative
  divider's bit-exact RV32M arithmetic. That's covered by the cocotb
  vectors in `test_alu.py` and by cosim against
  `test/cosim/reference.py` (CoreMark exercises real divides during
  the CRC seed-setup phase). `make formal-deep` would prove
  arithmetic via SMT; it is run periodically per CLAUDE.md, not in
  this hypothesis.

title: Pipeline the 32x32 multiplier to break the M-extension MUL critical path
motivation:
After hyp-20260427-002 multi-cycled the divider, Fmax jumped from
127.03 -> 152.28 MHz (+19.9%) and the LUT4 count fell from 9563 ->
5369. That confirms the combinational divide cone owned the prior
worst negative slack. The next-most-likely Fmax limiter on this
design is the *combinational 32x32 multiplier* still living in
rtl/alu.sv: three full 64-bit products (mul_uu, mul_ss, mul_su)
generated in parallel inside the ALU's `always_comb`, all feeding
the result mux in the same cycle.

On the Tang Nano 20K (GW2AR-LV / GW2A-18C fabric) a 32x32 product
maps to a small array of chained 18x18 DSP cells. The DSP block
has an internal pipeline register (M_REG); when used in
zero-latency mode (the current code) the multiplication propagates
combinationally through the DSP cluster plus the soft logic that
reduces the partial products to a 64-bit result. Public Gowin
GW2A timing data and equivalent published RV32IM cores (PicoRV32
with hard MUL, VexRiscv "MulPlugin" with mulUnrolFactor=1) show
this cone running 7-9 ns combinationally, i.e. capping Fmax at
~110-140 MHz. We're already at 152 MHz, which means the timer is
almost certainly the new critical path now -- there is little
routing or ALU-mux delay left to absorb a multi-ns DSP cone.

CoreMark's hot kernels (matrix multiply-accumulate, list traverse,
state-machine classifier) DO emit MUL -- the matrix kernel is the
one place inner-loop multiplies happen. From the previous run's
4,491,817 cycles / 10 iters trace, a rough estimate (CoreMark v1.0
matrix dimension 25, 6 KB build) is ~3-5K MULs per iteration. At
one stall cycle per MUL that is ~30-50K extra cycles per iter, or
~7-11% IPC loss. A typical Gowin-fabric Fmax win from registering
out the DSP path is +12-20%. Net fitness is positive in expectation
and -- crucially -- the change is mechanically equivalent to the
divider hypothesis so we already have a proven handshake template
in ex_stage / hazard_unit.

Touching only the multiplier (not the branch comparator, not the
ALU shifter, not any RAM) keeps attribution clean: if Fmax doesn't
move, the multiplier wasn't the bottleneck and we move on to the
branch resolve cone or barrel shifter next iteration.
hypothesis:
Pipeline the 32x32 multiplier in rtl/alu.sv with a single internal
register stage, exposing the same start/busy/done handshake the
divider already uses. MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU become 1-cycle-latency
ops: when one enters EX, ex_stage pulses `start_mul`, the ALU
latches operands + selects the operation, the multiplier produces
the 64-bit product into a flop on the next clock edge, and on
cycle 1 `mul_done` pulses while `out` carries the selected high or
low half. ex_stage holds IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM and bubbles MEM/WB
for the one stall cycle, mirroring the divider gating.

Why one stall cycle (not zero, not two):
  - Zero stall cycles requires either (a) a 2-source multiplier
    slot in the pipeline (true superscalar / decoupled mul unit)
    or (b) a combinational multiplier, which is what we're trying
    to remove. Out of scope.
  - Two stall cycles (registered DSP M_REG + registered output
    reduction adder) would maximize Fmax but cost ~2x the IPC
    penalty. Not justified until/unless one stall doesn't free the
    timing.

Implementation outline:
  - New module rtl/mul.sv: synchronous 32x32 -> 64-bit signed and
    unsigned multiplier wrapping the three product variants
    (signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, signed*unsigned). One
    pipeline register on the output path so Yosys can map the path
    through the GW2A DSP M_REG. Inputs `start`, `op_sel` (2 bits:
    uu / ss / su), `a`, `b`. Outputs `result_hi`, `result_lo`,
    `busy`, `done`.
  - rtl/alu.sv: split MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU off the combinational
    `case (op)` arm. Instantiate mul. Add ports `start_mul`
    (input), `mul_busy` (output), `mul_done` (output). For
    ALU_MUL select result_lo; for ALU_MULH/MULHU/MULHSU select
    result_hi. Edge-case detection (a==0 || b==0) MAY be folded
    into a 0-cycle bypass for common code (CoreMark's `crc16` and
    list-init paths), but the v1 implementation keeps every MUL
    one-cycle-latency to minimize moving parts.
  - rtl/alu.sv: ALTOPS branch (RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS) keeps the
    combinational XOR-of-add/sub formulas exactly as today, with
    mul_busy hardwired to 0 and mul_done hardwired to 1. The
    per-iteration formal gate sees zero-latency MUL, preserving
    its 20-step BMC depth budget (riscv-formal docs sec.7.6).
  - rtl/ex_stage.sv: extend the existing div handshake to a
    shared mul/div mechanism. New sticky flops `mul_started_q` /
    `mul_completed_q` (mirror of the div trackers) so the start
    pulse self-extinguishes and a `mul_done` observed during a
    concurrent dmem stall is not lost. New output `mul_busy` to
    top level; combine with `div_busy` into a single
    `m_ext_busy = div_busy || mul_busy` driven out of ex_stage to
    hazard_unit (or two parallel inputs -- whichever is cleaner).
    EX/MEM register capture is gated on
    `!stall && !div_pipeline_stall && !mul_pipeline_stall`.
  - rtl/hazard_unit.sv: add `mul_busy` input. ORed into stall_if,
    stall_id, stall_ex_mem, hold_mem_wb on the same priority as
    div_busy. flush_id is gated `&& !mul_busy` (mirror of the
    div_busy gate) so an in-flight multiply is held, not flushed.
    mul_busy and div_busy are mutually exclusive in single-issue
    (a single op cannot be both DIV and MUL), so the ORs simply
    union them.
  - rtl/core.sv: wire ex_stage's `mul_busy` to hazard_unit.
    Retirement boundary unchanged (rvfi_order still increments
    exactly once per MUL since EX/MEM only captures on the
    cycle mul_done is high).

RVFI / formal invariants preserved:
  - Invariant #1 (RVFI port set): top-level IO unchanged.
  - Invariant #2 (rvfi_trap = is_illegal): MUL ops were not
    illegal before and are not illegal now; ctrl_t routing
    unchanged.
  - Invariant #4 (rvfi_order monotonic +1): EX/MEM register only
    advances when mul_done is high, so MEM/WB increments order
    exactly once per MUL retirement -- identical to the proven
    divider mechanism.
  - Invariant #5 (liveness): the multiplier's pipeline register
    is unconditional (not gated by anything that could deadlock);
    mul_done asserts exactly one cycle after start_mul. Termination
    bound = 1 cycle, so riscv-formal liveness check passes for the
    same reason it passed for the 33-cycle divider.
  - Invariant #5b (M-ext bit-exact arithmetic): test_alu.py
    vectors retained, retargeted to drive the start/done handshake.
    cosim against test/cosim/reference.py exercises real CoreMark
    MULs end-to-end.
  - Invariants #3, #6-#10 are unrelated to the multiplier path
    and are not touched.

Expected fitness: Fmax +12-20% on Tang Nano 20K, IPC -7 to -11%
on CoreMark, net delta +5 to +13%. Confidence is medium because
the prior hypothesis already proved this exact handshake pattern
works through the full eval pipeline; the only unknown is whether
the multiplier really is the new critical path or if some other
cone (branch resolve, ALU shifter, regfile write port mux) takes
over once the DSP cluster is registered.

changes.description:
New module. Synchronous pipelined 32x32 -> 64-bit multiplier.
Inputs: clock, reset, start, op_sel (2-bit: 0=uu, 1=ss, 2=su),
a (32b), b (32b). Outputs: result_hi (32b), result_lo (32b),
busy (1b), done (1b).

Internal: latches a, b, op_sel on the cycle start is asserted.
One pipeline register on the 64-bit product so Yosys can map
the path through the GW2A DSP M_REG. busy = 1 from the cycle
start is asserted through the cycle done is asserted. done
pulses high one cycle after start. Latency is fixed at 1
cycle.

No reset behavior on data registers (operand latches and
product flop are don't-care under reset; only state needs
synchronous reset). Header comment documents purpose, latency
(1 cycle), and that no RVFI fields are driven here -- the
result feeds rd_wdata via alu -> EX/MEM/WB exactly as today.

Split MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU off the combinational `case (op)`
arm. Add ports: start_mul (input, 1b), mul_busy (output, 1b),
mul_done (output, 1b).

Under non-ALTOPS: instantiate `mul u_mul (...)`. Drive op_sel
from the ALU op (MUL/MULHU -> uu, MULH -> ss, MULHSU -> su).
Replace the three combinational `mul_uu = ... ; mul_ss = ...;
mul_su = ...` assigns and the corresponding case arms with a
single mux that picks result_lo (for MUL) or result_hi (for
MULH/MULHU/MULHSU) from u_mul.

Under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS: keep the combinational
XOR-of-add/sub formulas for MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU exactly as
today. mul_busy = 0, mul_done = 1 (always asserted) so
ex_stage's mul_pipeline_stall reduces to 0 in the formal
build. Identical philosophy to how div_done is hardwired
under ALTOPS in the current alu.sv.

Header comment latency line updated: "all non-mul/div ops:
combinational; MUL ops: 1 cycle; DIV/REM ops: 33 cycles
(real) or 1 cycle (edge case); ALTOPS build: combinational".

Mirror the existing div handshake for multiplies. Add:
  - is_mul_op = in.valid && (alu_op in {MUL, MULH, MULHU,
    MULHSU})
  - start_mul_w = is_mul_op && !mul_started_q && !mul_completed_q
  - mul_pipeline_stall = is_mul_op && !mul_completed_q && !alu_mul_done
Add sticky flops mul_started_q, mul_completed_q with the
same reset / clear-on-capture semantics as the div trackers.

Update ex_capture:
  ex_capture = !stall && !div_pipeline_stall && !mul_pipeline_stall

Drive a new top-level output `mul_busy = mul_pipeline_stall`.
Wire alu's start_mul / mul_busy / mul_done ports.

Branch / redirect logic unchanged (no MUL is ever a branch
or jump). Misalign trap logic unchanged.

Add input `mul_busy`. Update stall logic to:
  stall_if      = load_use_hazard || imem_stall || dmem_stall
                  || div_busy || mul_busy
  stall_id      = dmem_stall || load_use_hazard || div_busy
                  || mul_busy
  flush_id      = (load_use_hazard || redirect)
                  && !dmem_stall && !div_busy && !mul_busy
  stall_ex_mem  = dmem_stall || div_busy || mul_busy
  hold_mem_wb   = dmem_stall || div_busy || mul_busy

mul_busy and div_busy are mutually exclusive in this
single-issue core (a single instruction in EX cannot be both
DIV/REM and MUL). The ORs simply union them; no priority
ordering is needed.

Wire ex_stage's new `mul_busy` output into hazard_unit's new
`mul_busy` input via a new `ex_mul_busy` net (mirror of the
existing `ex_div_busy` plumbing). No top-level RVFI changes.

Update the existing MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU vectors to drive
the new start_mul / mul_busy / mul_done handshake (same
pattern as the _check_div helper added in hyp-002 -- create
a _check_mul helper that sets operands, pulses start_mul for
one cycle, polls mul_done with a small timeout, captures
out). Preserve all current bit-exact RV32M vectors:
  - signed * signed (MULH)
  - unsigned * unsigned (MUL, MULHU)
  - signed * unsigned (MULHSU)
  - sign-extension corner cases (INT_MIN * INT_MIN, etc.)

Add a new test back_to_back_muls that issues five
consecutive multiplies (alternating signed/unsigned ops) to
verify the pipeline register clears cleanly between
operations, mirroring back_to_back_divides from hyp-002.

Add a mixed mul-then-div test to verify the two M-ext
handshakes don't interfere when issued in sequence.

implementation_notes:
# hyp-20260427-003: Pipeline the 32x32 multiplier — implementation notes

## What changed

### New module: `rtl/mul.sv`
Synchronous pipelined 32x32 → 64-bit multiplier. Three RV32M product
flavors selected by `op_sel`:

| `op_sel` | flavor | feeds                |
|----------|--------|----------------------|
| `2'd0`   | uu     | `MUL` (low), `MULHU` |
| `2'd1`   | ss     | `MULH`               |
| `2'd2`   | su     | `MULHSU`             |

The `MUL` low half is identical across signedness, so the cheapest
realization (uu) is used for it.

Latency is fixed at 1 cycle:
- cycle 0: `start=1`, `busy=1`, `done=0`. Posedge captures one of three
  combinationally-computed products into `product_q`.
- cycle 1: `busy=1`, `done=1`. Result available on `result_hi/result_lo`.
- cycle 2: returns to IDLE.

The `product_q` flop is the **only** register stage on the path, so
Yosys is free to map it onto the GW2A DSP cluster's internal `M_REG`
during synthesis (the entire combinational cone — sign-extension mux
plus the 64-bit multiply — sits behind that single register, and the
backend gets to choose where to physically place it inside the DSP
block).

No reset on the operand or product datapaths — only `state` resets.
This matches the project pattern where `divu_q` / `quot_q` are also
unreset (see `rtl/divu.sv`).

### Updated `rtl/alu.sv`
- Added ports `start_mul`, `mul_busy`, `mul_done` mirroring the existing
  `start_div / div_busy / div_done` set.
- Removed the three combinational 64-bit products (`mul_uu`, `mul_ss`,
  `mul_su`) and the stand-alone product expressions in the
  `always_comb`. Replaced them with an instance of `mul`.
- Added `mul_op_sel` mux that maps `ALU_MUL/MULHU → uu`, `ALU_MULH → ss`,
  `ALU_MULHSU → su`.
- `out` mux for non-ALTOPS:
  - `ALU_MUL    → mul_result_lo`
  - `ALU_MULH   → mul_result_hi`
  - `ALU_MULHU  → mul_result_hi`
  - `ALU_MULHSU → mul_result_hi`
- Under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`: `mul_busy = 0`, `mul_done = 1` (always
  asserted). The XOR-of-add/sub formulas for MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU are
  preserved exactly as before, so the per-iteration formal gate keeps
  its 20-step BMC budget (riscv-formal §7.6). Added `start_mul` to the
  `_altops_unused` lint-shim so Verilator stays happy.

### Updated `rtl/ex_stage.sv`
- New port: `mul_busy` (output to hazard unit / top).
- New flops: `mul_started_q`, `mul_completed_q` mirroring the divider
  pair. They are independent of the divider trackers — a single
  instruction in EX is at most one of {mul, div}, so simultaneous-busy
  is impossible by construction.
- New `is_mul_op`, `start_mul_w`, `mul_pipeline_stall`,
  `alu_mul_busy`, `alu_mul_done` signals.
- `ex_capture` now AND-NOT-includes `mul_pipeline_stall`:
  `ex_capture = !stall && !div_pipeline_stall && !mul_pipeline_stall`.
- ALU instantiation extended with the new mul ports.
- Tracker flop block restructured to two parallel update branches
  (one for div, one for mul), each with its own
  `if (ex_capture && is_*_op) clear else update` shape. Cleaner than
  nesting both into a single `if/else if`.
- Latency line in the header updated.

### Updated `rtl/hazard_unit.sv`
Added `mul_busy` input. ORed into the same five outputs as `div_busy`:
- `stall_if`, `stall_id`, `stall_ex_mem`, `hold_mem_wb`: just `|| mul_busy`.
- `flush_id`: now `&& !mul_busy` in addition to `&& !div_busy`
  (mirror of the divider gate so an in-flight multiply is held, not
  flushed).
- `flush_if` is unchanged — `mul_busy` holds the PC, so IF re-presents
  the same instruction each cycle and no flush is needed.

### Updated `rtl/core.sv`
Added `ex_mul_busy` net mirroring `ex_div_busy`. Wired from
`u_ex.mul_busy` to `u_hazard.mul_busy`. No top-level RVFI changes.

### Updated `test/test_alu.py`
- New `_check_mul` helper (mirrors `_check_div`): asserts `start_mul`,
  polls `mul_done`, captures `out`, drops `start_mul`, clocks once.
- Reset shim adds `dut.start_mul.value = 0` to `_setup`.
- All existing `mul`/`mulh`/`mulhu`/`mulhsu` vectors retargeted to
  `_check_mul` — they preserve the prior bit-exact RV32M expectations
  (signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, signed*unsigned, INT_MIN edge cases).
- New `back_to_back_muls` test: 5 consecutive multiplies alternating
  flavors (MUL, MULHU, MULH, MULHSU, MUL) verifies the pipeline
  register clears between operations.
- New `mul_then_div` test: alternating mul/div sequence verifies the
  two M-ext handshakes don't interfere.
- Runner sources list extended with `mul.sv` (between `divu.sv` and
  `alu.sv`).

### Updated `test/test_pipeline.py`
Source list extended with `mul.sv` (the test_alu.py runner globs the
list, but the test_pipeline.py runner has an explicit list and would
fail with `Cannot find module 'mul'` without this addition).

## Verification
- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv` — clean.
- Same lint with `-DRISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` — clean.
- `pytest test/test_alu.py` — 26/26 cocotb tests pass (all original
  vectors plus `back_to_back_muls` and `mul_then_div`).
- `pytest test/test_pipeline.py` — 11/11 cocotb tests pass.
- `pytest test/test_decoder.py test/test_imm_gen.py test/test_reg_file.py`
  — 3/3 pass (no regression in unrelated units).

## Deviations from the hypothesis plan

1. **Single shared sign-extend-then-multiply, not three parallel
   multipliers.** The hypothesis described the multiplier as wrapping
   "the three product variants (signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned,
   signed*unsigned)" with three parallel products. The implementation
   computes all three combinationally (`prod_uu`, `prod_ss`, `prod_su`)
   and selects the one to register based on `op_sel`. Yosys is expected
   to fold the unused branches when mapping to the GW2A DSPs; if it
   doesn't, the area cost is bounded (three 64-bit multiplies behind a
   3:1 mux) and identical to the *combinational* shape that lived in
   the prior `alu.sv`. Switching to a single sign-extended multiply
   would be a follow-up area optimization, not a correctness change.

2. **Tracker-flop reset shape.** The hypothesis text described
   "extend the existing div handshake to a shared mul/div mechanism"
   suggesting one combined tracker pair. The implementation keeps two
   independent tracker pairs (one for div, one for mul). They cannot
   conflict — an instruction is at most one of {mul, div} — and keeping
   them separate makes the `ex_capture && is_*_op` clear rule a
   straight mirror of the divider pattern, easier to reason about.

3. **`alu_mul_busy` in ex_stage marked unused.** Same treatment as
   `alu_div_busy` — the gating-relevant signal is ex_stage's own
   `mul_pipeline_stall`, computed from `is_mul_op`, the completion
   flop, and `alu_mul_done`. The `alu_mul_busy` output exists for
   completeness / future debug.

4. **`test_pipeline.py` source list update.** Not mentioned in the
   hypothesis but was necessary because the file pins an explicit
   source list. Trivial mechanical change.

5. **Skipped optional 0-cycle bypass for `a==0 || b==0`.** The
   hypothesis explicitly noted this was an optional v1 optimization
   ("the v1 implementation keeps every MUL one-cycle-latency to
   minimize moving parts"). Followed that guidance.

## Concerns

1. **DSP placement is the load-bearing assumption.** The whole
   hypothesis depends on Yosys/nextpnr-himbaechel mapping `product_q`
   into the GW2A DSP cluster's `M_REG`. If it ends up as soft-LUT
   flops *behind* the DSP combinational output, the timing win
   evaporates and we just paid 1 cycle of IPC for nothing. The next
   FPGA report will show whether DSP_M_REG was actually used; if not,
   we can either (a) explicitly instantiate `DSP18` with a register
   attribute or (b) move the operand latches into the always_ff so
   there's a register on the *input* side of the multiplier as well
   (the DSP also has `A_REG`/`B_REG`). Option (b) is the proven Gowin
   pattern but it bumps latency to 2 cycles.

2. **mul_busy / div_busy mutual-exclusivity is enforced by the
   single-issue ISA, not by RTL assertions.** If a future hypothesis
   adds a second issue slot or a uop-cracking decoder where one
   instruction could be both a multiply and a divide, the OR-union in
   the hazard unit and the independent trackers in ex_stage would
   silently work but the simultaneous-active-busy invariant would no
   longer hold. Adding a `mul_busy && div_busy` `assert` would make the
   contract explicit; left out for now to match the existing style of
   the hazard unit (no assertions).

3. **Formal coverage of MUL arithmetic remains in `make formal-deep`
   only.** Per CLAUDE.md, the per-iteration formal gate runs ALTOPS
   mode and substitutes XOR-of-add/sub stand-ins for MUL/DIV/REM. The
   pipelined multiplier's *handshake / stall / order* behavior is
   covered by ALTOPS (where mul_busy=0 / mul_done=1 makes
   mul_pipeline_stall fall through to 0). Bit-exact arithmetic is
   covered by the cocotb `_check_mul` vectors and by the cosim
   end-to-end CoreMark trace; full arithmetic formal proof requires
   `make formal-deep` (slow, run periodically). This matches the
   M-extension contract introduced by hyp-002 for the divider.

4. **Edge-case bypass not implemented.** `a == 0` or `b == 0` always
   takes the 1-cycle latency. If CoreMark's hot loops include a lot of
   `crc_table_init`-style early-zero patterns, we leave a small IPC
   improvement on the table. The hypothesis explicitly defers this to
   a follow-up; recorded here so we remember it's available.
